data.title: Braskem: On The Edge
data.description:
Introduction

As some readers may know, I have a day job managing a Latam High Yield fund, and occasionally, my debt research has equity implications that I can share with Seeking Alpha. Braskem (NYSE:BAK) is one of these situations, where the Brazilian petrochemical company's equity and debt have been discounted as it struggles with a long-term bear market or demand/supply imbalance that has hit margins and driven the company to the edge.

What is BAK

Braskem is the world's 6th largest petrochemical company with plants in Brazil, the US, Germany, and Mexico that make polymers such as PVC, PE (Polyethylene), and PP (Polypropylene) that are called second generation, i.e., transformed from the building blocks of refined petroleum and NGL (natural gas liquids).

Braskem is also a basket case of corporate governance. Its former controller, Odebrecht, was convicted of corruption (car wash) and forced into insolvency, with the BAK shares confiscated by debt holders that are now selling to a turnaround/distressed investor. Then came an environmental catastrophe in Alagoas, Brazil (sinkholes) that has cost US$3.6bn. Finally, it's other controller, Petrobras (PBR), is its leading feedstock supplier, with its obvious conflicts of interest.

Petrochemical Market

The company is an intermediary petrochemical producer, converting raw material or feedstock such as naphtha (oil) or ethane (NGL) into a variety of polymers, such as PVC, that are then sold to end users. This means that its cost inputs are linked to oil prices while demand is driven by a broad-based industrial/consumer demand or GDP growth. The price of oil is inelastic and subject to geopolitical and OPEC inputs, while end products are far more sensitive to supply and demand inputs. BAK is a price taker on the cost and revenue side, which can result in large margin swings. The industry works on a spread vs oil cost to measure profitability; BAK should generally benefit from lower oil prices and an eventual oversupply of refining capacity.

The first chart is the price ratio of the company's three main products vs WTI, when that ratio is above 1x, Braskem (and most petrochemical companies) have a positive spread or margin vs oil. As can be seen, in 2011-2014 spreads were very tight due to the great recession that hurt plastics demand in a supply glut. A similar situation has been in effect since 2022 as China's slowdown has led to another glut. Still, spreads are marginally positive and have been inching higher, thanks to declining oil prices. However, demand in 2026 may be constrained due to the US trade war, which can lead to stagflation as the US consumer/business incorporates higher import taxes. This may reverberate throughout the world and keep polymer demand subdued.

Operating Model

I assume volumes in 2026 will be challenged to regain 2023/2024 levels as demand is slow to recuperate and the company operates at under 75% utilization to keep prices from declining. I assume average polymer prices are flat or up slightly in the US/EU, and cost inputs per ton are flat or decline on lower feed stock, which is 60% of COGS. For 2027-2028, I estimate volumes grow and prices increase while costs are flat, which drives gross margin back to over 20% and EBITDA margins above 10% with EBITDA more than doubling to over US$2.3bn, which is a more normalized level for Braskem.

Financial Estimates

The operating model above, while improving, is still weak with operating results (EBIT) less than financial expenses, resulting in net income losses through 2027. The company has around US$12bn in debt with financial expenses eating most of its cash flow, and BAK may not be positive FCF until 2027, assuming a rebound in demand/prices. Fortunately, most of its debt is long-term (after 2030), and through 2027, it does not have significant debt maturities that current cash cannot repay. The 2028 debt (Bonds) is likely to be called or rolled over in 2027.

Any hiccup in operating recovery toward 2027 may require a capital increase to get past the funding risk. This is when the Petrobras relationship comes in handy. However, suppose Tanure can acquire the controlling stake from the creditors. In that case, there is a risk that BAK forces a debt restructuring, converting bond interest payments into pay-in-kind (PIK) to generate cash flow.

Valuation

To estimate the BAK price target, one needs to believe that PVC, etc, prices will increase and the company, the global sector, will recuperate margins. Under this scenario, the stock could 4x to 2028, assigning a conservative 5x P/CE multiple. The consensus is a bit more aggressive. However, this upside potential may not start to be realized until the petrochemical cycle turns convincingly.

Peer Comps

I gathered consensus data on a broad range of chemical companies to compare growth, margins, and valuations vs Braskem. This a slow growth sector with improving margins as the consensus assumes chemical prices will recuperate in 2026, which leads to high cash earnings growth. Braskem has the lowest margin, impacted by high debt costs. Valuations are mixed; on a P/CE basis, the company is very cheap, while on EV/EBITDA, it is about 50% discounted, which may be due to the high debt levels and poor corporate governance.

Risk

If the petrochemical cycle prices do not turn and increase, the company will continue to burn cash and may require a capital increase, which in turn can drive the share lower. If the new controller embarks on a forced debt restructuring, the shares would suffer. Finally, as a Brazilian company, it may be subject to US import tariffs or other sanctions.

Conclusion

I rate BAK a hold. I would not buy the shares yet; I'd prefer to miss 30% but see the cycle turn before buying in. The company has a toxic risk-reward scenario; it could decline 50% on weak results, capital raise, and debt restructuring before the cycle turns.

data.title: DGS: Reasonable Yield And Geographical Diversification
data.description:
Opportunity Overview

The WisdomTree Emerging Markets SmallCap Dividend Fund (NYSEARCA:DGS) looks like one of the most attractive vehicles within emerging markets at the moment. It has a long-term history of outperforming many emerging market vehicles, including dividend-focused ETFs, and it offers uncorrelated returns since it does not track traditional benchmarks like the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. As a result, it is less vulnerable to pullbacks due to tensions in key markets like China and Taiwan, as well as pullbacks in large-cap EM stocks.

This ETF has outperformed the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) by over 40 percentage points in the past five years. This ETF gained significantly while many emerging markets struggled with inflation and other issues after 2022.

This article will explore how the country/sector/company composition of this ETF makes it a standout within emerging markets and even other dividend ETFs.

ETF Overview

The Wisdom Tree Emerging Markets Small Cap Dividend Fund is an excellent alternative vehicle in emerging markets, which stands out for its high dividend yield, low valuation, and unique portfolio composition. Investing in small-cap, high dividend yielding equities can be a solid strategy during risky economic cycles. This ETF offers unique exposure to emerging markets relative to other emerging market ETFs.

Small caps: None of the companies in this ETF have a market cap above $10 billion, and half of them have a market cap below $2 billion. Many of these stocks may be off the radar of most institutional investors and, most importantly, less vulnerable to drawdowns due to broader emerging market sell-offs or index rebalancing.

Superiority of Dividend Income: Investing in stocks with a high dividend yield can be an excellent inflation hedge and means to outperform, especially when these equities are undervalued. A study since 1957 posted by Wisdom Tree found that high-yielding stocks had a greater return per annum relative to the broader S&P 500 Index.

Valuation and Yield: At the moment, this ETF trades at a 13% discount to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index on a price to earnings basis, and a 28% discount on a price to book basis. It also has a notably higher dividend yield, as the MSCI Emerging Markets Index only has a 2.7% dividend yield.

Country Allocation: This ETF only invests around 35% of its assets in China and Taiwan, compared to 47% in MSCI Emerging Markets, and it is considerably underweight China. This is a notable improvement in terms of diversification, although it is still around 7 percentage points overweight Taiwan.

Other overlooked markets like Indonesia, Turkey, Chile, and the Philippines are some of the top 15 countries in this ETF, although they each account for less than 3% of this ETF.

This ETF also has a very unique country allocation relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index.

Below are some of my thoughts on this portfolio composition:

  • Taiwan: Taiwan will still face strong tariffs from the US economy, and was very vulnerable in 2023 due to the volatility of the semiconductor industry. The equity markets in Taiwan are up by around 12% this year, and could be vulnerable to pullbacks amid any new trade war tensions. Taiwan still looks like a good hold at the moment, especially since key companies may be exempt from Trump's 100% tariffs on semiconductors.
  • China: Chinese equities have also rallied this year despite trade war tensions, outperforming many dominant markets like Taiwan, South Korea, and other larger Asian markets. This ETF is slightly underweight China relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index, and also around 18 percentage points underweight relative to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. Being underweight China seems ideal now, especially following its H1 2025 rally.
  • India: Being underweight India may also lead to underperformance in the short term. Equities in this market already trade at 27x earnings and 3.8x book value, a hefty premium to other Asian emerging markets. India has also been one of the main targets of US tariffs due to its purchasing of Russian oil.
  • Other Overweight Decisions: This ETF is also significantly overweight undervalued markets like Brazil and Malaysia, which will lead to uncorrelated returns relative to other indexes.

Unique Industry Approach: One of the main drivers of emerging market performance has been from some of the dominant tech names in the market. This ETF takes a different approach by ignoring the noise and focusing on quality, high-yielding small caps in emerging markets.

Sectors like information technology and financials, which dominate many EM funds, take more of a back seat approach with this ETF, accounting for circa 31% of its assets. This allows for more exposure to consumer, real estate, and healthcare small caps, which are ignored by many larger funds that track the MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

Of course, there is a price to pay in the short term when emerging equities enter a bull market. Dominant emerging market names like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and HDFC Bank (HDB), several top 5 constituents of many emerging market funds, have outperformed this ETF.

However, this Wisdom Tree ETF has outperformed its emerging market benchmark peer, as well as many of the other dominant EM stocks, which have had massive drawdowns in the past five years. EM growth investing is all about timing and choosing the best names, while this Wisdom Tree ETF is a more surefire bet, which has still delivered a stellar 64% return in the past five years.

EM Performance Varies

There has been a massive divergence in the performance of emerging markets this year, with a notable surprise from many Asian markets that have defied trade war tensions, as they did during Trump's previous administration.

China and Taiwan have been two of the key drivers of positive performance in emerging Asian markets, which has largely explained the bull run in emerging market ETFs this year.

While this ETF has been a relative underperformer this year, it may be a safer bet in H2 2025 if larger emerging equity markets experience pullbacks, especially since many of these ETFs are heavily concentrated in large caps, with over half of their assets invested in a small handful of markets.

A Unique Vehicle Poised to Outperform Benchmark

A comparison of the long-term performance of this ETF to other dividend ETFs shows two trends:

1) This ETF has been a superior long-term vehicle relative to other emerging market dividend funds, outperforming many other dividend funds. This ETF, and even other Wisdom Tree ETFs, look like a clear victor if you are focused on emerging market dividend investing.

2) Standard dividend funds, which primarily focus on US equities, have trounced this ETF in the past decade.

The top-performing ETF, the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VYM), has a much lower yield at the moment and primarily focuses on major large caps in the United States.

When comparing this ETF to U.S.-focused ETFs, the picture is a bit more murky. These Vanguard vehicles have been driven more by capital gains, and currently offer a much lower dividend yield.

The S&P 500 has also trounced these companies in terms of capital gains. In essence, these ETFs have functioned more like safer, growth ETFs.

If one is focused on emerging markets, this WisdomTree Emerging Market Smallcap Dividend Fund looks like one of the safer bets in the market, as it has historically outperformed and may be less vulnerable to drawdowns. This ETF has had a solid run in the past couple of years, resulting in a slightly lower yield at the moment. However, it may not be the best international dividend ETF. The iShares International Dividend ETF (IDV), which has also performed in line with this ETF, is an attractive vehicle for dividend investors that offers a slightly higher yield at the moment.
